elements.sent-2411-001: We study a class of random matrices arising from the Lax matrix structure of classical integrable systems, particularly the Calogero family of models.
elements.sent-2411-002: Our focus is the density of eigenvalues for these random matrices.
elements.sent-2411-003: The problem can be mapped to analyzing the density of eigenvalues for generalized versions of conventional random matrix ensembles, including a modified form of the log-gas.
elements.sent-2411-004: The mapping comes from the underlying integrable structure of these models.
elements.sent-2411-005: Such deep connection is confirmed by extensive Monte-Carlo simulations.
elements.sent-2411-006: Thereby we move forward not only in terms of understanding such class of random matrices arising from integrable many-body systems, but also by providing a building block for the generalized hydrodynamic description of integrable systems.
elements.sent-2411-007: Their inherent mathematical structure provides a unique avenue for deeper analytical understanding.
elements.sent-2411-008: Simple examples of integrable systems are non-interacting particles and harmonic chains.
elements.sent-2411-009: In the former, the energy of each particle is conserved while in the latter the energy associated with normal modes is conserved, thereby resulting in an extensive number of conserved quantities.
elements.sent-2411-010: When interactions or non-linearities are introduced, mixing among these non-interacting components causes the loss of essentially all local conservation laws leading to non-integrable chaotic systems~\cite{arnold, kam .}
elements.sent-2411-011: However, upon careful fine-tuning, the system may maintain the large number of conserved charges \cite{olshanetsky1981classical,babelon2003introduction,cao1990classical,doyon2017dynamics .}
elements.sent-2411-012: Notable examples include hard rod systems \cite{tonks1936complete,percus1969exact,boldrighini1983one , the Toda chain \cite{toda1967vibration, ford1973integrability, flaschka1975, toda1989theory, toda1989studies, spohn2020generalized, spohn2021hydrodynamic and Calogero models \cite{polychronakos1992, polychronakos2006physics, kulkarni2017emergence, gon2019duality, calogero1969solution, calogero1969ground, calogero1971solution, sutherland1971exact, sutherland1972exact, sutherland1975exact, abanov2011soliton .}}}
elements.sent-2411-013: Somewhat apart is the family of integrable classical spin models, as the Ishimori chain \cite{ishimori, haldane and the Abolwitz Ladik chain \cite{spohn2022, ALbook, brollo2024 .}}
elements.sent-2411-014: For classical systems, establishing the existence of an extensive number of conservation laws is mostly based on the availability of a Lax pair, $L$ and $M$ \cite{olshanetsky1981classical .}
elements.sent-2411-015: These are $N \times N$ matrices whose entries depend on positions $\{q_i\}$ and momenta $\{p_i\}$ of $N$ particles.
elements.sent-2411-016: For such integrable systems Newton's equations of motion can be recast as \begin{equation}\dot{L} = [L, M],\label{Ldot}\end{equation} where $[A, B] = AB-BA$ denotes the commutator.
elements.sent-2411-017: From Eq.~(\ref{Ldot ), one deduces that the eigenvalues $\{\lambda_i\}$ of $L$ are time-independent~\cite{olshanetsky1981classical .}}
elements.sent-2411-018: However eigenvalues as a function of $\{q_i\}$, $\{p_i\}$ are non-local.
elements.sent-2411-019: For the purpose of a hydrodynamic description charges must have a local density.
elements.sent-2411-020: This is achieved by defining the charges $Q_k = \mathrm{tr [L^k]$, $k=1,...,N$, where $\rm{tr [A]$ denotes the trace of the matrix $A$.}}
elements.sent-2411-021: These charges are conserved and have a quasi-local density.
elements.sent-2411-022: The Lax formalism is a crucial tool not only for establishing an extensive number of locally conserved charges but also to provide an algebraic method to handle them.
elements.sent-2411-023: Recently, there has been a growing interest in the hydrodynamics of integrable systems both theoretically \cite{doyon2020lecture, essler2022short, castro2016emergent, bertini2016transport, bouchoule2022generalized, doyon2023generalized, doyon2018geometric and experimentally \cite{malvania2021generalized, schemmer2019generalized .}}
elements.sent-2411-024: For classical integrable systems, a central tool is the empirical density of eigenvalues (density of states, DOS) of the Lax matrix.
elements.sent-2411-025: The DOS is defined as \begin{equation}\label{LaxDOS0} \varrho_N(\lambda) = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^N \delta(\lambda - \lambda_i),\end{equation} where the $\{\lambda_i\}$ are the eigenvalues of the Lax matrix.
elements.sent-2411-026: If positions and momenta are randomly distributed, then $L$ becomes a random matrix.
elements.sent-2411-027: Hence its eigenvalues, $\{\lambda_i\}$, are random and $\varrho_N(\lambda)$ turns into a random probability measure.
elements.sent-2411-028: A specific example is the case where the positions and the momentum are distributed according to the thermal Gibbs-Boltzmann distribution \begin{equation}P(\{q_i, p_i\}_{i=1}^N)= \frac{1}{Z} \exp\left(-\beta H(\{q_i, p_i\})\right),\end{equation} at inverse temperature $\beta$, where $Z$ is the normalizing partition function.
elements.sent-2411-029: When these positions and momentum are entries of the Lax matrix then we refer to such density of eigenvalues as the thermal Lax DOS.
elements.sent-2411-030: In the context of a hydrodynamic description of integrable systems, the Generalized Gibbs ensemble (GGE) is of central importance.
elements.sent-2411-031: In this case, one expects that \begin{equation}\label{LaxDOS} \lim_{N \to \infty}\varrho_N(\lambda) = \varrho(\lambda)\end{equation} with probability one in the thermodynamic limit.
elements.sent-2411-033: To compute a limiting DOS is an extensively studied problem in the context of Random Matrix Theory, starting with Wigner ensembles~\cite{wigner1951 .}
elements.sent-2411-034: In the same spirit, it is of interest to study the DOS of eigenvalues associated with the Lax matrix $L$ \cite{bogomolny2009random from integrable classical Hamiltonians.}
elements.sent-2411-035: For short, we call them random Lax matrices.
elements.sent-2411-036: They are part of classical integrable many-body theory and constitute an interesting novel class of random matrices.
elements.sent-2411-037: In this article, we consider the hyperbolic Calogero model, which consists of $N$ particles interacting via a short-range repulsive pair-wise potential.
elements.sent-2411-038: The Hamiltonian reads \cite{olshanetsky1981classical \begin{equation}\label{hcm: PE} H_{c} \big(\{q_i, p_i\}\big) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\tfrac{1}{2} p_i^2 + \sum_{1\leq i < j}^N V_{c}(q_i-q_j), \quad \quad V_{c}(r) = \frac{1}{4\sinh^2\left(r/2\right)}.\end{equation}.}
elements.formula-2411-005: \begin{equation}\label{hcm: PE} H_{c} \big(\{q_i, p_i\}\big) = \sum_{i=1}^{N}\tfrac{1}{2} p_i^2 + \sum_{1\leq i < j}^N V_{c}(q_i-q_j), \quad \quad V_{c}(r) = \frac{1}{4\sinh^2\left(r/2\right)}.\end{equation}

elements.sent-2411-042: Therefore we refer to $H_{\mathrm{c }$ as Calogero fluid~\cite{olshanetsky1981classical .}}

elements.sent-2411-044: The central agenda of this article is to study the DOS of the Lax matrix $L_{\mathrm{c }$ in case the system is in Gibbs thermal equilibrium.}
elements.sent-2411-045: For this purpose one has to confine a system of $N$ particles to a region approximately of size $\ell$ and then take the limit $N,\ell \to \infty$ at fixed particle density $\bar{\rho =N/\ell$.}
elements.sent-2411-046: The asymptotic DOS is expected to be independent of the precise confining mechanism.
elements.sent-2411-047: As discussed in \cite{spohn2023hydrodynamic , an analytically very powerful choice is the fine-tuned external potential \begin{equation}\label{vex} U_C(\{q_i\}) = \sum_{i=1}^N e^{-\ell/2} \cosh(q_i).\end{equation}.}

elements.sent-2411-066: In our article, we numerically investigate the Lax DOS in Gibbs thermal equilibrium (thermal Lax DOS) of the Calogero fluid for a wide range of densities and temperatures.
elements.sent-2411-067: We also elucidate the dependence on boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-068: Our main contributions are as follows:.
elements.sent-2411-069: Using the Monte Carlo simulations we compute the thermal Lax DOS of the Calogero fluid for three boundary conditions (a) the cosh confining trap, (b) the confining box and (c) on a ring.
elements.sent-2411-070: For the case of cosh confinement we compute the Lax DOS using two methods: by direct diagonalization and based on the exact joint distribution of eigenvalues, see~\cite{spohn2023hydrodynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-071: For the other two cases (b) and (c) we use the method based on direct diagonalization of the Lax Matrix.
elements.sent-2411-072: To elucidate the role of the boundary conditions, we compare the Lax DOS of the Calogero fluid for different boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-073: We demonstrate that, at low density the Calogero fluid is well approximated by the Toda chain and at high density by the rational Calogero model.
elements.sent-2411-074: For these parameters we compare the DOS of the Calogero fluid with a direct numerical simulation of the approximating models in different boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-075: The article is organized as follows.
elements.sent-2411-076: In Section~\ref{sec:blg we will present some details of the Calogero fluid and review the derivation of the joint distribution of the Lax matrix eigenvalues.}
elements.sent-2411-077: In Section~\ref{sec: num , we present our numerical findings on the Calogero fluid.}
elements.sent-2411-078: Section~\ref{sec:spl is devoted to low and high density limits of the Calogero fluid.}
elements.sent-2411-079: We summarize our results along with an outlook in Section~\ref{sec:conc .}
elements.sent-2411-080: Some details are delegated to the Appendix.

elements.section-2411-003: Numerical study of the Lax DOS
elements.sent-2411-102: Since the interaction potential is short-ranged, the equilibrium state has a finite correlation length and one expects the DOS to be independent of boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-103: To obtain a global overview of the DOS as a function of the particle density $\bar \rho$ and the temperature $T =\beta^{-1}$, we first use the box potential of length $\ell$ given in Eq.~\eqref{vbox .}
elements.sent-2411-104: Then the Boltzmann weight is given by \begin{equation}\label{B} \exp\big(-\beta H_{\mathrm{c}}\left(\{q_i, p_i\}\right) - U_\mathrm{B}\left(\{q_i\}\right)\big).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-105: The configurations $\{q_i,p_i\}$ are sampled by a Monte Carlo (MC) algorithm.
elements.sent-2411-106: For each configuration the corresponding Lax matrix $L_\mathrm{c}$ is diagonalized, thereby obtaining the DOS.
elements.sent-2411-107: Interestingly, specific limits of the Calogero fluid sill define integrable many-particle models, on which we elaborate below.
elements.sent-2411-108: When the particle density is low, $\bar \rho \ll 1$, then the typical gap $\nu = 1/\bar \rho$ between particles is large, $\nu \gg 1$.
elements.sent-2411-109: At this scale, the interaction potential of the Calogero fluid approximately behaves as an exponentially decaying function of the interparticle distance $r$.
elements.sent-2411-110: Furthermore, the nearest-neighbor interaction exponentially dominates the next nearest-neighbor one.
elements.sent-2411-111: Thereby one arrives at the Toda chain for which particles with neighbouring index interact through a potential of the form \begin{equation}\label{Vtoda} V_{to}(r) = \exp(-r).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-112: Here the subscript ``to'' stands for Toda chain.
elements.sent-2411-113: The Toda chain is an integrable system \cite{toda1967vibration, olshanetsky1981classical and its Lax DOS has been studied when the system is confined to an external trap ~\cite{spohn2023hydrodynamic .}}
elements.sent-2411-114: Therefore, at a given low particle density the Toda chain and the Calogero fluid are expected to display similar equilibrium properties like Lax DOS.
elements.sent-2411-115: We provide a brief discussion of the Lax DOS for the Toda chain in \ref{ldosptoda .}
elements.sent-2411-116: On the other hand, when the density of particles is high, $\bar \rho \gg 1$, particles are very close.
elements.sent-2411-117: At this scale, the interaction potential of the Calogero fluid behaves as an inverse quadratic function of interparticle distance.
elements.sent-2411-118: This is the interaction potential of the rational Calogero model where the particles repel each other with a power-law potential of the form \begin{equation}\label{Vrational} V_{r}(r) = \frac{1}{r^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-119: Here the subscript ``r'' stands for rational Calogero model.
elements.sent-2411-120: The rational Calogero model is also an integrable system \cite{calogero1969solution, polychronakos2006physics, olshanetsky1981classical .}
elements.sent-2411-121: When these particles are confined to a ring of length $\ell$, the periodized interaction Eq.~\eqref{Vrational results in the following pair potential \begin{equation}V_{t}(r) = \frac{1}{\sin^2\left(\frac{\pi}{\ell}r\right)},\end{equation} which is known trigonometric Calogero model \cite{choquardclassical, olshanetsky1981classical .}}
elements.sent-2411-122: Here the subscript ``t'' refers to the trigonometric form of the interaction.
elements.sent-2411-124: The Lax DOS of the trigonometric Calogero model is well understood analytically using the Thermodynamic Bethe ansatz (TBA)~\cite{spohn2023hydrodynamic .}
elements.sent-2411-125: We briefly discuss the Lax DOS for this model in \ref{trig .}
elements.sent-2411-126: The Lax DOS of the Toda chain and the trigonometric Calogero model will be used to benchmark our findings for the Lax DOS of the Calogero fluid.

elements.sent-2411-200: To summarize, we study a new class of random matrices which arise from the Lax matrix structure of classical integrable systems.
elements.sent-2411-201: As in Random Matrix Theory (RMT), the density of states (DOS), i.e. the density of eigenvalues, is the first and most natural quantity that is investigated.
elements.sent-2411-202: For the Calogero model confined in a box, we thoroughly investigate the DOS of the random matrix that emerges from its Lax matrix constructed from the phase space configurations sampled from the Boltzmann-Gibbs distribution.
elements.sent-2411-203: We demonstrate how the shape depends on the density of Calogero particles and their temperature, see Fig.~\ref{fig:dos_vs_T_rbar .}
elements.sent-2411-204: Remarkably, as demonstrated in Ref.~\cite{spohn2023hydrodynamic , if the particles of the Calogero fluid are confined in an external cosh potential, the joint probability distribution of the eigenvalues of the Lax matrix of the Calogero fluid [Eq.~\eqref{Lax: hcm ] can be mapped to a modification of the conventional log-gas at high temperatures [Eq.~\eqref{poflambda0 ].}}}
elements.sent-2411-205: In this work, we have provided compelling numerical verification of this via extensive MC simulations.
elements.sent-2411-206: In Fig.~\ref{fig:comparbessinh , we show an excellent match between the density of eigenvalues obtained from the modified log-gas and from directly diagonalising the Lax matrix of the Calogero fluid.}
elements.sent-2411-207: Next, we address the question about the sensitivity of the Lax DOS to the details of the boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-208: To do so we consider three different boundary conditions: (i) an external trap of cosh type [Eq.~\eqref{vex ], (ii) the confining box with hard walls and (iii) on a ring.}
elements.sent-2411-209: We provide compelling numerical evidence that in the thermodynamic limit, the DOS is independent of the specific boundary conditions used, see Fig.~\ref{fig:comparsinhweier .}
elements.sent-2411-210: This finding is consistent with the intuition that for the short-range models such as the Calogero fluid the role of boundary conditions is unimportant due to fast decaying correlations.
elements.sent-2411-211: The Hamiltonian for the Calogero fluid at low and high density limits corresponds to that of well-studied integrable models namely the Toda chain and the rational Calogero model, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-212: Consequently, one would expect the Lax DOS to match the respective limits, thus providing a nice procedure to extract the Lax DOS of these various deeply connected integrable models.
elements.sent-2411-213: In Fig.~\ref{fig:todalowd , we present a comparison of the Lax DOS of the cosh-confined Calogero fluid in the low density of particles limit with that of the Toda model where we observe a good match.}
elements.sent-2411-214: Similarly, in Fig.~\ref{fig:comparratsinh we compare the Lax DOS of the cosh-confined Calogero fluid at a relatively high density with that of the cosh-confined rational Calogero model where we again observe a good match.}
elements.sent-2411-215: In both cases, we study various boundary conditions and establish insensitivity to the details of the boundary condition in the thermodynamic limit.
elements.sent-2411-216: Our work is an important step forward in two main ways.
elements.sent-2411-217: Firstly, it puts forward a new class of random matrices arising from integrable models which may be amenable to analytical results for a variety of conventional random matrix diagnostics.
elements.sent-2411-218: For example, one could further explore quantities such as level spacing statistics~\cite{forrestor2010, haake1991, mehta2004 , adjacent gap ratio~\cite{huse2007 and spectral form factor~\cite{prakash2021,prasad2023, cotler2017, gharibyan2018, liu2018 to unravel the signatures of short and long-range correlations between eigenvalues in this new class of random Lax matrices.}}}
elements.sent-2411-219: Secondly, the random Lax matrices, we investigated are also an important building block for generalized hydrodynamic descriptions of integrable systems.
elements.sent-2411-220: Although, in this work, we focused on an integrable particle model, it would be interesting to explore integrable lattice discretizations of continuum integrable field theories that possess Lax pair structure.
elements.sent-2411-221: Examples of such promising platforms are the Ablowitz-Ladik equation~\cite{ALbook,spohn2022 and integrable discretization of the Landau-Lifshitz model \cite{LL1,LL2,LL3 .}}

elements.section-2411-007: Lax DOS of the Toda Chain
elements.sent-2411-242: In this appendix, we discuss the Lax DOS of the Toda chain when the system is in thermal equilibrium at inverse temperature $\beta$ and constant pressure $P$.
elements.sent-2411-243: Such ensemble was studied in Ref.~\cite{spohn2020generalized , where it was shown that the Lax DOS is related to the following free energy functional \begin{equation}\label{free: ptoda} F_{P}(\rho) = \int_{-\infty}^{\infty} d\lambda~\tfrac{1}{2}\beta\lambda^2\rho(\lambda)+ \log P-P \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}\int_{-\infty}^{\infty}d\lambda~d\lambda'~\log\left(|\lambda-\lambda'|\right)\rho(\lambda)\rho(\lambda') \notag + \int_{-\infty}^{\infty}d\lambda~\rho(\lambda)\log\rho(\lambda).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-244: This is the free energy of the conventional log-gas.
elements.sent-2411-245: In RMT one uses a scaling such that energy dominates entropy.
elements.sent-2411-246: However, in Eq.~\eqref{free: ptoda energy and entropy are of the same order.}
elements.sent-2411-247: In the terminology of RMT, Eq.~\eqref{free: ptoda then corresponds to high temperatures.}
elements.sent-2411-248: The free energy Eq.~\eqref{free: ptoda is derived for the Toda chain with a linear pressure ramp.}
elements.sent-2411-249: Thus to obtain the Toda DOS one first has to minimize Eq.~\eqref{free: ptoda over all normalized probability densities $\rho$.}
elements.sent-2411-250: Denoting this unique minimizer by $\rho^*(\lambda)$, the Toda Lax DOS, $\varrho(\lambda)$, is given by \begin{equation}\label{dos: ptoda} \varrho(\lambda) = \partial_P\left(P\rho^*(\lambda)\right).\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-251: In fact, in the case of thermal equilibrium, an exact expression for $\rho^*$ has been derived in ~\cite{allez2012, opper1985 with the result \begin{equation}\label{ptoda: theory} \rho^*(\lambda) = \frac{1}{\sqrt{2 \pi} \Gamma(1+P)}\frac{1}{|D_{-P}(i\lambda)|^2}\end{equation} where the function $D_{-P (z)$ has the following integral representation \begin{equation}D_{-P}(z) = \frac{1}{\Gamma(P)}\exp\left(-\tfrac{1}{4}z^2\right)\int_{0}^{\infty}dx~x^{P-1}\exp\left(-z\,x-\tfrac{1}{2}x^2\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-252: To compare the Lax DOS of the Toda chain [Eq.~\eqref{dos: ptoda ] under constant pressure and the confined Toda chains with fixed particle density $\bar{\rho = \frac{1 {\nu}$ we use the following relation to compute the pressure \begin{equation}\label{relationpressurestretch} \frac{1}{\bar{\rho}} = \nu = \langle r\rangle_{P} = \frac{\partial F_{{to}}}{\partial P},\qquad F_{{to}} = \partial_{P}\left(PF_{P}(\rho^*)\right)-(1+2P)\log(2).\end{equation}.}}}
elements.sent-2411-253: Recall that the subscript ``$\mathrm{to}$'' in Eq.~\eqref{relationpressurestretch represents the Toda interaction.}
elements.section-2411-008: Lax DOS of the trigonometric Calogero model
elements.sent-2411-254: We discuss the Lax DOS of the trigonometric Calogero model when the system is in thermal equilibrium at inverse temperature $\beta$ and confined to a ring of length $\ell$ through periodic boundary conditions.
elements.sent-2411-256: By using action-angle coordinates it is shown that the thermal Gibbs distribution can be expressed as \begin{equation}\label{p-trig} \frac{1}{Z(\beta, \ell)}\exp\left(-\beta H_{t}\left(\{q_i, p_i\}\right)\right)\prod_{i=1}^Ndp_idq_i =\frac{1}{Z(\beta, \ell)}\exp\left(-\tfrac{\beta}{2}tr[L_{t}^2]\right)\chi(\{\lambda_i\})\prod_{i=1}^Nd\lambda_id\phi_i,\end{equation} where subscript ``$\mathrm{t}$'' represents the trigonometric Calogero model.
elements.sent-2411-257: Here $\{\phi_i\}$ are the angle variables taking values on the ring, $0<\phi_i<\ell$.
elements.sent-2411-258: The action variables, $\{\lambda_i\}$, are the eigenvalues of the Lax matrix $L_{\mathrm{t }$.}
elements.sent-2411-259: They are constrained such that $\lambda_{i+1 -\lambda_i\geq 2\pi/\ell$ for $1\leq i\leq N-1$, which can be expressed as \begin{equation}\chi(\{\lambda_i\}) = \prod_{i=1}^{N -1}\Theta\left(\lambda_{i+1}-\lambda_i-\tfrac{2\pi}{\ell}\right),\end{equation} using the Heavyside function $\Theta(x) = 1$ for $x>0$ and $\Theta(x)=0$ otherwise.}
elements.sent-2411-260: The partition function can then be expressed as \begin{equation}Z(\beta, \ell) = \ell^N \int~\prod_{i=1}^Nd\lambda_i\exp\left(-\tfrac{\beta}{2}\lambda_i^2\right) \prod_{i=1}^N\Theta\left(\lambda_{i+1}-\lambda_i-\tfrac{2\pi}{\ell}\right),\end{equation} which is exactly the thermal partition function at temperature $\beta^{-1}$ of hard rods of length $2\pi/\ell$ confined by a harmonic trap with trap strength $1$.
elements.sent-2411-261: As shown in \cite{spohn2023hydrodynamic , the thermodynamic limit of the Lax DOS is given by \begin{equation}\varrho^*(\lambda) = \frac{f^*(\lambda)}{(1+2\pi f^*(\lambda))},\end{equation} where the function $f^*(\lambda)$ satisfies the following transcendental equation ~\cite{choquardclassical \begin{equation}\label{saddle-trig} f^*(\lambda) = \exp\left(-2 \pi f^*(\lambda)\right)\exp\left(-\tfrac{\beta}{2}\lambda^2 + \mu\right).\end{equation}.}}
elements.sent-2411-262: The function $f^*(\lambda)$ is the derivative of the free energy density of the harmonically trapped hard rods in the thermodynamic limit.
elements.sent-2411-263: Here the chemical potential $\mu$ is fixed through the normalization constraint, $\int d\lambda~\varrho^*(\lambda)=1$.
elements.sent-2411-264: In Fig.~\ref{fig:comparratsinh , we compare the Lax DOS obtained by solving Eq.~\eqref{saddle-trig with those obtained from MC simulations of the Calogero model and the rational Calogero model at high densities.. }}
